text: This chapter integrates information from many lines of evidence to consider past, present and future changes in the global water cycle. Building on the analysis of past changes and projected future hydrological changes in Chapters 2, 3 and 4, as well as on the discussion of past and future climate regime changes and climate extremes in Chapters 10 and 11, the physical processes underlying these changes are discussed here together with an analysis of the changes and their causes, their projections and their associated uncertainties, and possibilities of abrupt changes in the water cycle. The integration of information from paleoclimatic studies, present day observations, atmospheric reanalyses and global and regional climate models has revealed large changes to the components of the global water cycle that are primarily caused by anthropogenic activities. These changes were sometimes obscured during the 20th century by the natural climate fluctuations that largely controlled the climate signal of the time; these fluctuations will become much less important in the coming years when the anthropogenic forcing is likely to become dominant.
explanation: The passage uses formal, broad phrasing and cross-chapter references typical of scientific reports, which can be produced by AI or by careful human authors. The lack of concrete data or citations makes AI authorship plausible but not certain.
